questions.q: What is the definition of thermodynamics?
questions.answer: Thermodynamics is the branch of physics that deals with the relationships between heat, work, and energy. It involves the study of the transfer of energy from one body to another due to a temperature difference. Thermodynamics is concerned with the macroscopic properties of systems, such as temperature, pressure, and volume.

questions.q: State the zeroth law of thermodynamics.
questions.answer: The zeroth law of thermodynamics states that if two systems are in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are also in thermal equilibrium with each other. This law allows us to define a temperature scale, such as the Celsius or Kelvin scale.

questions.q: What is the difference between internal energy and heat?
questions.answer: Internal energy is the total energy of a system, including both kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in the system. Heat, on the other hand, is the transfer of energy from one body to another due to a temperature difference. Internal energy is a property of the system, while heat is a process.

questions.q: State the first law of thermodynamics.
questions.answer: The first law of thermodynamics, also known as the law of energy conservation, states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another. Mathematically, it is expressed as ΔU = Q - W, where ΔU is the change in internal energy, Q is the heat added to the system, and W is the work done by the system.

Enthalpy (H) is a thermodynamic property that is defined as the sum of the internal energy (U) and the product of the pressure (P) and volume (V) of a system. Mathematically, it is expressed as H = U + PV. Enthalpy is often used to describe the energy of a system at constant pressure.

questions.q: State the second law of thermodynamics.
questions.answer: The second law of thermodynamics states that the total entropy (a measure of disorder or randomness) of an isolated system always increases over time. Mathematically, it is expressed as ΔS = ΔQ / T, where ΔS is the change in entropy, ΔQ is the heat added to the system, and T is the temperature at which the heat is added.

questions.q: What is the difference between isothermal and adiabatic processes?
questions.answer: An isothermal process is a process in which the temperature of the system remains constant. An adiabatic process, on the other hand, is a process in which no heat is transferred between the system and its surroundings. In an adiabatic process, the temperature of the system may change.

The Carnot efficiency (η) is the maximum efficiency of a heat engine operating between two temperature reservoirs. It is given by the equation η = 1 - (Tc / Th), where Tc is the temperature of the cold reservoir and Th is the temperature of the hot reservoir.

questions.q: What is the difference between reversible and irreversible processes?
questions.answer: A reversible process is a process that can be reversed without any change in the surroundings. An irreversible process, on the other hand, is a process that cannot be reversed without some change in the surroundings. Reversible processes are ideal and do not occur in reality.

Entropy (S) is a measure of the disorder or randomness of a system. It is a thermodynamic property that is related to the amount of thermal energy unavailable to do work in a system. Entropy is often used to describe the energy of a system at constant temperature.
